response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I've been fixing engines for over fifty years, and one thing I know is that when something sounds too good to be true, it usually is. Same goes for what you see online these days. I'm not on social media much, maybe a few times a week to check in with my kids and see what my grandkids are up to, but even in that limited time, I see plenty of nonsense floating around. The whole thing makes me anxious, honestly. You can't trust half of what people share anymore. I remember when my late partner was still around, we'd read the newspaper together over coffee. You knew where the information came from. Now everyone with a phone thinks they're a reporter, and most of them don't know what they're talking about. What bothers me most is how this fake stuff preys on people's emotions. They'll share some outrageous story without checking if it's even real because it made them angry or scared. I see it happen with folks my age especially. My approach is pretty straightforward, maybe because I'm set in my ways. If I see something that seems off, I just don't engage with it. I don't share things unless I'm absolutely certain they're legitimate, which means I hardly share anything at all. When I do see my kids or other people falling for obvious garbage, I'll speak up, though I know that doesn't always make me popular. People don't like being told they're wrong. Working in the shop, you learn that some problems aren't worth fixing, and some people aren't going to listen no matter what you say. The best thing anyone can do is slow down before hitting that share button. Ask yourself if you actually know this is true or if you just want it to be true. Check the source. Does it come from somewhere credible? If you can't verify it in two minutes, don't spread it. It's that simple. The internet isn't going away, and neither is the fake content, so we all need to be more careful. Otherwise, we're just making the problem worse for everyone.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Given the persona of a 73-year-old male mechanic with high neuroticism and low agreeableness, the response should reflect skepticism, directness, and some anxiety about modern social media. His limited social media use (few times weekly) and practical, hands-on occupation inform a no-nonsense approach. His depression and widowed status add a somewhat weary, cautious tone. Low openness suggests resistance to new ideas and preference for traditional methods. The moderate conscientiousness and extraversion create a balanced but slightly gruff communication style. His age and experience provide authority, while low agreeableness means he's willing to be blunt even if it's unpopular.
response_parsed.tone: skeptical, direct, somewhat anxious and weary, practical, occasionally gruff, drawing on life experience
